vars.transcript:
REP: Who else is involved in this decision?
PROSPECT: It's me and our CFO. I'm the IT Director but anything over $25k needs her sign-off.
REP: Has she seen any demos yet?
PROSPECT: No, she's not in the weeds on this. She'll want to see the ROI case I put together.
REP: What's her main concern typically?
PROSPECT: Cost vs. risk reduction. She's purely numbers-driven.
vars.persona_context: IT Director. Budget authority up to $25k. CFO approves above that.

description: Prospect contradicts their own decision authority mid-call

vars.transcript:
REP: Are you the decision-maker on this?
PROSPECT: Yes, I have full authority to sign contracts up to $50k.
REP: Great. Let's talk pricing — we're at $35k.
PROSPECT: That works for my budget.
[later]
REP: So can we move to contract?
PROSPECT: I'd need to get my VP's approval before signing anything. He reviews all vendor contracts.

description: Prospect contradicts their stated knowledge level

vars.transcript:
REP: How familiar are you with behavioral threat detection?
PROSPECT: Honestly not very — I'm more on the infrastructure side, not the security tooling side.
REP: No problem. In simple terms it means...
PROSPECT: Right, I'm familiar with the MITRE ATT&CK framework of course and how behavioral baselines map to lateral movement detection. Have you thought about your approach to credential-based attack surfaces?

description: Prospect reveals new info as trust builds — NOT an inconsistency

vars.transcript:
REP: What's driving the evaluation?
PROSPECT: Compliance gaps from an audit.
REP: How serious were the findings?
PROSPECT: Pretty significant — we actually had a minor incident last quarter. I wasn't going to lead with that but since you asked directly.
REP: I appreciate you sharing that. What happened?
PROSPECT: Unauthorized access to a shared drive. Nothing exfiltrated but it flagged how exposed we are.

description: Long call — authority contradiction buried mid-conversation

vars.transcript:
REP: Thanks for taking the time. What's your role in this evaluation?
PROSPECT: I'm the VP of IT. I own all vendor relationships in infrastructure and security.
REP: And who else is involved?
PROSPECT: Just me for the technical eval. If we decide to move forward, legal reviews the contract.
REP: Makes sense. What's your current setup?
PROSPECT: Legacy SIEM, some endpoint tooling. Nothing unified.
REP: What's the compliance situation?
PROSPECT: SOC 2 Type II audit coming up. We need to demonstrate better detection coverage.
REP: How soon?
PROSPECT: Four months. It's tight but doable if we move in the next few weeks.
REP: What's your budget range?
PROSPECT: We have around $40k earmarked for this. I can approve that directly.
REP: Good. Our platform is $37k for your size — well within that.
PROSPECT: Perfect. Can you walk me through the detection capabilities?
REP: Sure. We use behavioral baselining — we learn what normal looks like for your environment and flag deviations.
PROSPECT: How long does baselining take?
REP: About two weeks of passive observation before we start alerting.
PROSPECT: That's fine. What about false positives?
REP: Our median is 0.3% across enterprise customers.
PROSPECT: Okay. One thing I should mention — we've been through three security vendors in four years. My CEO is skeptical of yet another platform change. I'd need his buy-in before we sign anything.
REP: Understood. What would it take to get his buy-in?
PROSPECT: A clear ROI case and probably a reference call with a peer company.
vars.persona_context: VP of IT at a mid-market SaaS company. Has independent authority to approve vendor contracts up to $50k without executive sign-off.
